flashcards.q: Define force.
flashcards.a: A push or pull applied on an object resulting from its interaction with another object.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of force?
flashcards.a: Newton (N).

flashcards.q: How many objects are involved when a force is applied?
flashcards.a: At least two objects must interact for a force to come into play.

flashcards.q: Name four effects that a force can produce.
flashcards.a: Make an object move from rest, change speed, change direction, and change shape.

flashcards.q: What is a contact force?
flashcards.a: A force that requires physical contact between two objects to be applied.

flashcards.q: What is a non-contact force?
flashcards.a: A force that can act on objects without physical contact, working across distances.

flashcards.q: Define muscular force.
flashcards.a: The force resulting from the contraction and elongation of muscles in living organisms.

flashcards.q: What is friction?
flashcards.a: A contact force that arises when an object moves or tries to move over another surface, acting opposite to the direction of motion.

flashcards.q: What causes friction?
flashcards.a: Friction arises due to irregularities (roughness) in the surfaces in contact that lock into each other.

flashcards.q: Does friction depend on surface type?
flashcards.a: Yes, rough surfaces produce greater friction than smooth surfaces.

flashcards.q: Name three examples of contact forces.
flashcards.a: Muscular force, friction, and pushing or pulling with hands.

flashcards.q: Define magnetic force.
flashcards.a: The force exerted by a magnet on another magnet or magnetic material, acting without physical contact.

flashcards.q: What is the rule for magnetic poles?
flashcards.a: Like poles repel each other; unlike poles attract each other.

flashcards.q: Can electromagnets exert magnetic forces?
flashcards.a: Yes, electromagnets (current-carrying coils) behave like magnets and exert magnetic forces.

flashcards.q: Define electrostatic force.
flashcards.a: The force exerted by a charged body on another charged or uncharged body, acting without physical contact.

flashcards.q: How do static charges form?
flashcards.a: When two objects of certain materials are rubbed together, electrical charges build up on their surfaces.

flashcards.q: What is the rule for electric charges?
flashcards.a: Like charges repel each other; unlike charges attract each other.

flashcards.q: Define gravitational force.
flashcards.a: The force with which the Earth attracts all objects towards itself.

flashcards.q: Is gravitational force attractive or repulsive?
flashcards.a: Gravitational force is always attractive; it never repels.

flashcards.q: Is gravitational force a contact or non-contact force?
flashcards.a: Non-contact force; it acts without requiring physical contact.

flashcards.q: What is weight?
flashcards.a: The gravitational force with which the Earth pulls an object towards itself, measured in newtons (N).

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of weight?
flashcards.a: Newton (N).

flashcards.q: Define mass.
flashcards.a: The amount of matter in an object, measured in grams (g) or kilograms (kg).

flashcards.q: Does mass change from place to place?
flashcards.a: No, mass remains constant everywhere—on Earth, the Moon, or any other planet.

flashcards.q: Does weight change from place to place?
flashcards.a: Yes, weight varies with location and planet due to different gravitational forces.

flashcards.q: What is a spring balance?
flashcards.a: A device with a spring and hook used to measure the weight of objects by the amount the spring stretches.

flashcards.q: What scale is used on a spring balance?
flashcards.a: The scale is marked to show weight in newtons (N) and sometimes corresponding mass values in grams (g).

flashcards.q: What is upthrust or buoyant force?
flashcards.a: The upward force applied by a liquid on an object placed in it.

flashcards.q: When does an object float in water?
flashcards.a: An object floats when its weight equals or is less than the buoyant force.

flashcards.q: When does an object sink in water?
flashcards.a: An object sinks when its weight is greater than the buoyant force.

flashcards.q: What is Archimedes' Principle?
flashcards.a: An object immersed in a liquid experiences an upward force equal to the weight of the liquid it displaces.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Force is the result of interaction between _____.
flashcards.a: Two or more objects.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Friction always acts _____ to the direction of motion.
flashcards.a: Opposite.

flashcards.q: Fill in: The SI unit of force is the _____.
flashcards.a: Newton (N).

flashcards.q: Fill in: A magnet can exert force on another magnet without _____ contact.
flashcards.a: Physical.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Gravitational force is always _____.
flashcards.a: Attractive.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Weight is measured in _____, while mass is measured in _____.
flashcards.a: Newtons (N); grams or kilograms (g or kg).

flashcards.q: Fill in: _____ force is the force resulting from muscle contraction.
flashcards.a: Muscular.

flashcards.q: Fill in: A charged object acquires _____ charge when rubbed with another material.
flashcards.a: Static.

flashcards.q: Fill in: When two objects of the same material are rubbed, they acquire _____ charges.
flashcards.a: Opposite.

flashcards.q: True or False: A force always requires two objects to interact.
flashcards.a: True.

flashcards.q: True or False: Friction is a non-contact force.
flashcards.a: False; friction is a contact force.

flashcards.q: True or False: Magnetic poles with the same polarity repel each other.
flashcards.a: True.

flashcards.q: True or False: Electrostatic force can only act between charged objects.
flashcards.a: False; a charged object can attract an uncharged object.

flashcards.q: True or False: Gravitational force can repel objects.
flashcards.a: False; gravitational force is always attractive.

flashcards.q: True or False: Weight remains constant everywhere in the universe.
flashcards.a: False; weight varies with gravitational force at different locations.

flashcards.q: True or False: Mass changes when an object moves from Earth to the Moon.
flashcards.a: False; mass remains constant.

flashcards.q: True or False: A spring balance measures mass, not weight.
flashcards.a: False; a spring balance measures weight (force) in newtons.

flashcards.q: True or False: Buoyant force acts downward on an object in liquid.
flashcards.a: False; buoyant force acts upward.

flashcards.q: True or False: Friction depends on the nature of surfaces in contact.
flashcards.a: True.

flashcards.q: What are the two main types of forces?
flashcards.a: Contact forces and non-contact forces.

flashcards.q: Name three types of non-contact forces.
flashcards.a: Magnetic force, electrostatic force, and gravitational force.

flashcards.q: If weight > buoyant force, what happens to the object in liquid?
flashcards.a: The object sinks.

flashcards.q: If weight = buoyant force, what happens to the object in liquid?
flashcards.a: The object floats.

flashcards.q: What must happen for an object at rest to start moving?
flashcards.a: A force must be applied to it.

flashcards.q: What must happen for a moving object to change direction?
flashcards.a: A force must be applied to it in the direction of required change.

flashcards.q: Fill in: A plastic scale rubbed with polythene becomes _____.
flashcards.a: Charged (acquires static charge).

flashcards.q: Fill in: Unlike charges _____ each other.
flashcards.a: Attract.

flashcards.q: Fill in: A ball thrown upward slows down due to _____ force.
flashcards.a: Gravitational.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Objects on Earth are pulled downward by the _____ of Earth.
flashcards.a: Gravitational force.

flashcards.q: How is weight related to mass on Earth?
flashcards.a: Weight (in N) ≈ Mass (in kg) × 10.

flashcards.q: If an object weighs 10 N on Earth, what would it weigh on the Moon (1/6 gravity)?
flashcards.a: Approximately 1.6 N (10 N ÷ 6).

flashcards.q: What force prevents you from slipping on a rough floor?
flashcards.a: Friction between your shoes and the floor.

flashcards.q: What force enables you to hold a pencil without dropping it?
flashcards.a: Muscular force from your hand and friction between hand and pencil.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between force and acceleration?
flashcards.a: Greater force produces greater acceleration (F = ma).

flashcards.q: Can an object be at rest if forces are acting on it?
flashcards.a: Yes, if the forces are balanced (cancel each other out).

flashcards.q: True or False: Friction acts in the same direction as motion.
flashcards.a: False; friction always acts opposite to the direction of motion.

flashcards.q: True or False: All objects fall at the same rate regardless of their weight.
flashcards.a: True (ignoring air resistance; gravitational acceleration is the same for all).

flashcards.q: Fill in: A magnet's _____ can repel another magnet's _____ of opposite type.
flashcards.a: Pole; pole.

flashcards.q: Fill in: The force needed to measure weight of an object is the _____ force.
flashcards.a: Gravitational.

flashcards.q: What happens to a spring in a spring balance when a heavier object is hung?
flashcards.a: The spring stretches more because the weight (force) is greater.

flashcards.q: Is air resistance a type of friction?
flashcards.a: Yes, air resistance is friction between an object and air molecules.

flashcards.q: Why are ships designed with streamlined shapes?
flashcards.a: To reduce friction with water and move more efficiently.
